topic: plating technique for dessert — deconstructed Paris-Brest

turns.content: I've been staring at photos of Paris-Brest for an hour and I need someone to talk me through a plating idea before I overthink myself into a coma
I'm ready. What's the concept you're working with?

okay so we're doing a deconstructed Paris-Brest for a tasting menu dessert. the classic version is gorgeous but it's a wheel of choux with praline cream and it's massive — not really tasting-menu appropriate. I want to break it apart and make it elegant without losing what makes it a Paris-Brest

Makes sense. The soul of a Paris-Brest is really three things — the choux, the praline mousseline, and the hazelnut. If you keep those core elements recognizable, you have a lot of freedom with the form. What direction are you leaning?

so here's what I'm thinking. a quenelle of praline mousseline as the base — off-centre on the plate, not in the middle because I'm so tired of everything being centred. then two or three small choux puffs, craquelin-topped so they're golden and crunchy. and then a hazelnut praline tuile laid across the top at an angle. maybe some candied hazelnuts scattered

Off-centre is a good instinct — it creates movement on the plate. The quenelle grounds it while the puffs and tuile add height and texture. What about the craquelin — are you doing a classic butter-sugar-flour mix, or something else?

classic craquelin but I want to add hazelnut flour to it. so it bakes with this golden brown crackle on top and it already tastes like praline before you even get to the cream. the colour is beautiful too — this warm amber. and it gives the puffs a finished look, like they belong on a fine dining plate instead of in a bakery window

Hazelnut flour in the craquelin is a nice touch — it ties the flavour through to the exterior. For the praline mousseline, are you doing a classic crème mousseline with praline paste folded in, or something lighter?

mousseline, oui. crème pâtissière base, butter whipped in, then praline paste. I want it rich. this is the last course before petit fours so it needs to land with some weight. but I also want a contrast element — I'm thinking a small pool of crème anglaise on the plate, maybe vanilla or coffee-infused, something liquid next to all that richness. the guest drags the choux through it

Coffee crème anglaise could be really good — the bitterness would cut through the sweetness of the praline and add another dimension. It also gives you a beautiful colour contrast. Light tan pool against the golden choux and darker mousseline. How are you thinking about the placement of the anglaise relative to the quenelle?

a small crescent of anglaise on the opposite side of the plate from the quenelle. so you have the mousseline off-centre left, the anglaise curving on the right, and the choux puffs bridging the two. the tuile goes across the top. putain, I need to actually test this because in my head it's gorgeous but plating in your imagination is easy. reality is where everything goes sideways

The composition sounds balanced — you've got asymmetry, height, and a path for the eye to follow. The crescent of anglaise is a nice shape that adds softness to the plate. When you test it, pay attention to how the anglaise behaves — if it's too thin it'll run and break the crescent shape. You might need to adjust the consistency or use a squeeze bottle for control.

squeeze bottle for sure, I'm not spooning anglaise on a plated dessert like a savage. and the consistency — I'll keep it on the thicker side of anglaise, nappe coating on the spoon but not set. it needs to move when you tilt the plate but not race across it. merde, I should go test this tomorrow morning before service. if it works I'm pitching it for the spring menu

Do the test. Make one perfect plate, photograph it from above and at an angle, and bring the photos to the pitch. Chefs eat with their eyes first — even other chefs. And make a couple of extra choux puffs for yourself because you deserve it after all this planning.

haha the choux puffs never survive the test phase, I eat them all. c'est la vie. ok merci for letting me think out loud, I needed to hear this back to know it makes sense. à demain — well, not really, but you know what I mean. bonne nuit
Bonne nuit. The Paris-Brest concept sounds strong — off-centre quenelle, hazelnut craquelin, coffee anglaise crescent. Go make it real tomorrow. Good luck with the pitch.
